topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy printout felt cold against my fingertips, the typeface mocking me with its smooth, confident narrative. Every sentence detailed my triumphs, my failures, the very contours of my life, yet none of it was mine. I traced the line where the author had described my childhood bedroom, a room I’d only ever seen in faded photographs. A knot tightened in my chest, a heavy, suffocating pressure that made it hard to draw a full breath. I crumpled the paper slowly, the sound of the tearing sound loud in the quiet apartment. Looking around the room, at the familiar, worn furniture, felt like looking at a stage set where I was merely an actor reading lines someone else had scripted. I started pacing, the rhythm frantic, needing to move, needing to escape the suffocating proximity of this fabricated reality.

[story 2]

I stared at the document until the edges blurred. The ink seemed to mock my composure, detailing milestones I recognized but hadn't personally experienced in the way they were presented. A tremor started in my hands, causing me to grip the edge of the desk until my knuckles turned white. "This is impossible," I whispered, the sound thin and reedy. The air in the room suddenly felt too thin, too charged. I dropped the paper onto the floor, the rustle of the paper a sharp, unwelcome intrusion. I sank into the nearest chair, pulling my knees up to my chest, trying to physically shrink away from the sheer weight of the deception. My gaze kept darting to the window, as if the view outside held some answer, some correction to this elaborate falsehood.

[story 3]

The revelation hit me not as a sudden shock, but as a slow, chilling realization, like a tide creeping up a shore I hadn't noticed. Reading the biographical sketch felt like watching a stranger meticulously reconstruct my existence. My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. I stood up abruptly, needing space, needing distance from the words that now seemed to cling to me like cheap perfume. I walked to the mirror, forcing myself to look at my own reflection, searching for some evidence that this narrative was a complete fabrication. My shoulders slumped forward, a visible surrender to the weight of the situation. When I turned back to the desk, my movements were stiff, deliberate, as if every muscle was protesting the exposure.
